title: Voluntary Sustainable Apparel Labeling Act
plain_summary: This law tells the EPA to create a program where clothing companies can choose to put special labels on their clothes. These labels will show customers how much pollution the clothes make during their whole life, from when they are made until they are thrown away. The program will have rules about what information goes on the labels and how to check that the information is correct. Companies that use fake labels or lie about their clothes will get in trouble.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Voluntary Sustainable Apparel Labeling Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Voluntary Sustainable Apparel Labeling Program
sections.text:
The head of the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) must create a voluntary program for labeling clothes based on how they affect the environment. The EPA will work with the Department of Agriculture and the Federal Trade Commission to set up this program. The EPA has two years to write the final rules for how this program will work.

Any person who sells clothes can ask the EPA to include their clothes in this program. The EPA will decide what information people need to include when they apply. The EPA will review each application and decide whether to let the clothes into the program. This decision will be based on whether the EPA thinks the seller will follow the program rules, not on whether the clothes are actually good for the environment.

If the EPA approves clothes for the program, the seller can put a special label on the clothes or their packaging. The EPA will decide what this label must look like. The label must give consumers information that helps them make buying decisions. It cannot say whether clothes are good or bad, but must give numbers showing environmental information. The label must use words, numbers, and pictures chosen by the EPA. It must show that the EPA has checked the information. It must include a logo so people know it is part of this program.

The label must show a number summary of the total greenhouse gas pollution created during the entire life of the clothes. This includes pollution from growing plant fibers, making materials, making the clothes, packaging, shipping, storing, selling in stores, washing and ironing by customers, and throwing away or recycling the clothes at the end. The label must also show any promises the seller made about being more environmentally friendly. The label must provide a QR code or similar way for customers to get more detailed information right away using their phone.

If the person putting the label on is the manufacturer and not the store selling to customers, this must be clearly shown on the label. If requested, the pollution information can be split into two parts. The seller reports information about growing materials, making materials, making clothes, packaging, and shipping. The EPA estimates information about storing, selling, customer use, and disposal based on typical use patterns. If information is split this way, the label must clearly show which part came from which source.

Only information chosen by the EPA can be put on the label. The EPA must set up a way to check that all information on labels is accurate. This checking process must use the same standards for all clothes, use the best available science, and specify requirements for who can measure and verify information. The checking process must follow international standards for measuring carbon pollution throughout a product's life, including ISO standards and greenhouse gas accounting rules from international business groups. The process must also follow current best practices used in the clothing industry.

When setting up label requirements, the EPA must talk with a group of clothing industry experts. The EPA can do this by creating a formal advisory committee or through special rule making processes that involve negotiation.

The EPA must create a program to certify organizations that can check the information for labels. Starting seven years after this law takes effect, and every five years after that, the EPA must publish a report on how well the program is working. The EPA must look at whether the program gives consumers useful information and reduces greenhouse gas pollution from clothes. The EPA must review and change the rules to make the program work better. The EPA must tell Congress about any changes to this law that would improve the program.

The EPA must provide technical help to people who want to participate in the program, people who want to become participants, and organizations that want to become certified to check information. This help includes advice on reporting information, participating in the program, and reducing greenhouse gas pollution.

The EPA must create a program where sellers can make voluntary promises to reduce greenhouse gas pollution from their clothes throughout the entire life cycle. Sellers can also promise to make sustainability information available to the public on their websites, as long as this information is based on the best available science. The EPA has two years to set up this voluntary promise program. The EPA must encourage people to make these promises and help them do so. However, the EPA cannot force anyone to make promises.

The EPA must create a database within two years that gives easy access to information about the labeling program. This database must include explanations of the program goals and methods, information about each piece of clothing in the program, voluntary promises made by sellers, and any other sustainability information about clothes that would be useful to consumers and industry members and is based on the best available science.

Within three years, the EPA must create a program to teach consumers about the labeling program. This program must give stores educational materials to share with customers, provide technical help to stores, and reach the public through many different ways including public service announcements and advertising.

If someone breaks the rules of this program, they must pay a penalty of up to ten thousand dollars for each violation. Each separate violation counts as a separate offense. If someone keeps violating the rules after the EPA tells them to stop, each day they continue counts as a separate offense. Federal courts can order people to stop violating the rules and provide other appropriate remedies.

This section defines important terms. Apparel industry stakeholders means suppliers, manufacturers, retail stores, organizations that check information, colleges and universities, consumer groups, and other experts chosen by the EPA. Greenhouse gas includes carbon dioxide, methane, nitrous oxide, sulfur hexafluoride, various fluorinated compounds, and other specified chemicals. Greenhouse gas emission means releasing greenhouse gases into the air. Retail apparel establishment means a person who sells clothes to consumers.
